lists.title: Arabic A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 Modern Standard Arabic in communicative order: pronouns, verbless nominal sentences, the definite article with sun and moon letters, gender and ة, demonstratives, adjective agreement, the root-and-pattern system, iḍāfa possession, attached pronouns, the present tense, negation, and questions.

points.title: Personal pronouns (أنا، أنتَ، هو…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: أنا (I), أنت (you — masculine anta, feminine anti), هو (he), هي (she), نحن (we), هم (they). Arabic distinguishes masculine and feminine 'you' by the final vowel — same letters, different sound.

points.title: Nominal sentences — no 'to be'
points.function: Describe and identify without a verb
points.explanation: Arabic drops 'to be' in the present: البيت كبير (the house [is] big), أنا سعيد (I [am] happy). Subject + predicate, nothing in between — a complete, correct sentence.

points.title: The definite article ال — sun and moon letters
points.function: Point to a specific thing
points.explanation:
ال attaches to the noun: بيت (a house) → البيت (the house).

Before 'sun letters' (ت، د، س، ش، ص، ن…) the ل assimilates in speech: الشارع sounds ash-shāriʿ, not al-shāriʿ. Before 'moon letters' (ب، ك، م، ق…) the ل is pronounced: القمر = al-qamar.

Writing never changes — only the sound.
points.culture_note: The names come from الشمس (the sun — assimilating) and القمر (the moon — not). The alphabet splits exactly in half: 14 sun, 14 moon.

drills.hint: sun + ال (the L assimilates — sun letter)

points.title: Gender and ة (tāʾ marbūṭa)
points.function: Name people by who they are
points.explanation:
Nouns are masculine or feminine; the letter ة at the end usually marks feminine: معلم (male teacher) → معلمة (female teacher), طالب → طالبة.

Most profession and role words come in this pair.

points.title: Demonstratives (هذا / هذه)
points.function: Introduce and point at things
points.explanation:
هذا (this — masculine), هذه (this — feminine, and for the ة words). With an indefinite noun it makes a sentence: هذا كتاب (this is a book); with a definite noun it points: هذا الكتاب (this book…).

For things farther away: ذلك (m) and تلك (f) — ذلك الكتاب (that book), تلك المدينة (that city).

drills.translation: That man over there is my brother.
That car is a new car.

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Describe things correctly
points.explanation:
The adjective FOLLOWS the noun and copies it in gender AND definiteness: بيت كبير (a big house), البيت الكبير (the big house), سيارة جديدة (a new car — feminine ة on both).

Two words, matching uniforms.

drills.translation: The beautiful city is far away.
I am reading a new book.

points.title: Roots and patterns (ك ت ب)
points.function: See how Arabic words are built
points.explanation:
Most Arabic words grow from a three-consonant root poured into patterns.

From ك-ت-ب (writing): كتاب (book), كاتب (writer — the 'doer' pattern), مكتب (office/desk — the 'place' pattern), مكتبة (library).

Learn to spot the root and unknown words start explaining themselves.
points.culture_note: Arabic dictionaries are organized by root, not by spelling — to look up مكتبة you go to ك-ت-ب.

drills.hint: the thing you read — root k-t-b

drills.translation: The office is near the house.
drills.hint: the place pattern of k-t-b + the article
the book-place — feminine ة + the article

points.title: Iḍāfa — possession by pairing
points.function: Say whose or of what something is
points.explanation:
Two nouns side by side make 'the X of Y': كتاب الطالب (the student's book), باب البيت (the door of the house). The FIRST noun never takes ال — definiteness comes from the second.

No word for 'of' at all.

drills.hint: student + the article — the owner comes second

drills.translation: The door of the house is open.

points.title: Attached pronouns (كتابي، كتابك…)
points.function: Say my, your, his — in one word
points.explanation:
Possessives attach to the noun: كتابي (my book), كتابك (your book), كتابه (his), كتابها (her).

Feminine ة opens into ت before the suffix: سيارة → سيارتي (my car).

The pattern is iḍāfa again — with a pronoun as the owner.

drills.translation: Our car is in front of the house.

points.title: Present tense (أدرس، يعمل…)
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation:
The present conjugates with prefixes: أ (I) — أدرس (I study), ت (you/she) — تدرس, ي (he) — يدرس, ن (we) — ندرس.

'They' adds an ending too: يدرسون. Prefix first, root inside.

drills.translation: I go to school every day.

drills.hint: to drink, you (m.) — same form as 'she'

drills.translation: He drinks coffee in the morning.

points.title: Negation (لا / ليس)
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation: Two negators, two jobs: لا before a present verb (لا أفهم — I don't understand), and ليس for nominal sentences (هو ليس هنا — he is not here). ليس conjugates: لستُ (I'm not), ليست (she's not).

points.title: Questions (هل، ما، من، أين)
points.function: Ask about things, people, places, and time
points.explanation: ما (what), من (who), أين (where), متى (when), كيف (how) — and هل, which turns any statement into a yes/no question: هل تدرس العربية؟ (Do you study Arabic?). Question word first, sentence unchanged after it.

points.title: Past tense (الماضي)
points.function: Talk about completed actions
points.explanation:
The past tense (al-māḍī) starts from the bare 3rd-person masculine form — دَرَسَ darasa 'he studied' — and adds a subject suffix: -at (she, دَرَسَتْ), -nā (we, دَرَسْنا), -tum (you pl., دَرَسْتُم), -ū (they, دَرَسوا).

The 'I' and singular 'you' forms add only a final short vowel (دَرَسْتُ darastu, دَرَسْتَ darasta), so they look identical unvowelled. Unlike the present, the past has no subject prefix.

drills.translation: The student studied a lot yesterday.
The girl studied for the exam.
We studied together in the library.
Yesterday we studied the whole lesson.

points.title: The future (سـ / سوف)
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation: The future prefixes the present-tense verb with سـ (sa-, attached) for the near future or سوف (sawfa, separate) for a more distant or emphatic future: سَيَكْتُب sa-yaktub 'he will write', سوف أذهب sawfa adhhab 'I will go'. Negate the future with لن + subjunctive, not with these particles.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to school.
He will write a letter to his friend.
You will know the truth one day.
She will work at the company next year.

points.title: Sound plurals (ون/ين، ات)
points.function: Make regular plurals of people and many nouns
points.explanation:
The sound masculine plural adds ـون (-ūn) in the subject case and ـين (-īn) elsewhere, mostly for male humans: مُعَلِّم → مُعَلِّمون.

The sound feminine plural replaces ة with ـات (-āt): مُعَلِّمة → مُعَلِّمات, and works for many non-human nouns too (سيّارات cars).

Irregular nouns take a broken plural instead.

drills.translation: The teachers are at the school.
I saw the teachers at the conference.
The nurses (f.) work at the hospital.
The magazines are on the table.

points.title: Broken plurals (جمع التكسير)
points.function: Recognize irregular plural patterns
points.explanation:
Most Arabic nouns pluralize by reshaping the word around the root rather than adding a suffix — the 'broken' plural.

There are fixed patterns: كِتاب → كُتُب (kitāb → kutub), وَلَد → أَوْلاد (walad → awlād), بَيْت → بُيوت (bayt → buyūt), قَلَم → أَقْلام (qalam → aqlām).

You memorize the plural with each noun, but the patterns recur.

drills.translation: I read three books this month.
The boys are playing in the garden.
There are three pens on the desk.
We visited several cities in Egypt.
There are many men in the forest.

points.title: The dual (المثنّى)
points.function: Talk about exactly two of something
points.explanation:
Arabic has a distinct form for exactly two: add ـان (-ān) in the subject case or ـين (-ayn) elsewhere — كِتاب → كِتابان (two books), طالِب → طالِبان (two students).

Feminine nouns keep the ة before the ending: سيّارة → سيّارَتان.

Verbs and adjectives agree in the dual too.

drills.translation: Two students came to the class.
I saw two men in front of the house.
The table has only two legs.
I have two watches on my hand.

points.title: Prepositions (حروف الجر)
points.function: Say where, from where, and to where
points.explanation:
Prepositions (ḥurūf al-jarr) put the following noun in the genitive (-i / -in).

The core set: في fī 'in', على ʿalā 'on', من min 'from', إلى ilā 'to', عن ʿan 'about', مع maʿa 'with', and the attached بـ bi- 'with/by' and لـ li- 'for/to'.

Many verbs pair with a fixed preposition.

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
I am in the house now.
We are going to the airport.

points.title: Comparative & superlative (أفعل التفضيل)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
The comparative uses the pattern أَفْعَل (afʿal) from the adjective's root, invariable for gender, followed by مِن 'than': كبير → أَكْبَر مِن 'bigger than'.

The same form becomes a superlative when it's made definite or heads an iḍāfa: الأَكْبَر / أَكْبَر مدينة 'the biggest / the biggest city'.

drills.translation: The elephant is bigger than the cat.
This road is shorter than that one.
Arabic is harder than English for me.
Cairo is the biggest city in Egypt.
He is the smartest student in the class.
Health is more important than money.

points.title: kāna — 'was / were' (كان)
points.function: Put a nominal sentence in the past
points.explanation:
A verbless sentence is put in the past with كان kāna.

It keeps the subject in the nominative but makes the predicate accusative (its 'khabar'): الطقسُ جميلٌ → كان الطقسُ جميلاً 'the weather was nice'. كان agrees with the subject: كان (he), كانت (she), كانوا (they).

drills.translation: The city was quiet in the morning.
The students were tired after the exam.
The players were happy with the win.

points.title: Object pronouns on verbs (رأيتُه، رأيتُها…)
points.function: Attach the object directly to the verb
points.explanation:
The object of a verb can attach to it as a suffix instead of a separate word: رأى raʾā 'he saw' → رآه raʾāhu 'he saw him', رآها raʾāhā 'saw her', رآهم raʾāhum 'saw them', رآني raʾānī 'saw me', رآك raʾāka 'saw you'.

The suffixes are the same object pronouns used after nouns and prepositions.

drills.translation: The book is useful; I read all of it.
I liked the film, so I watched it twice.
The story is nice; I heard it from my grandmother.
The car is old but I love it.
My friends are here; I invited them home.
The guests arrived, so I welcomed them warmly.

points.title: Numbers 3–10 (العدد والمعدود)
points.function: Count things from three to ten
points.explanation:
For 3–10 the counted noun is a plural in the genitive, and the number shows reverse gender agreement (polarity): a masculine noun takes the feminine number and vice versa — ثلاثة كُتُب thalāthat kutub (3 masc. books, feminine number) but ثلاث بنات thalāth banāt (3 fem. girls, masculine number).

The number 'eats' the ة against the noun's gender.

drills.translation: I have three books on the shelf.
There are only three girls in the class.
Five guests visited us in the evening.
There are five fingers on the hand.
I read six magazines this week.

points.title: The subjunctive (المنصوب)
points.function: Say what you want, will, or aim to do
points.explanation: After أَنْ (that), لَنْ (will not), حَتّى / كَيْ / لِـ (so that, in order to), the present verb goes into the subjunctive: the final ُ becomes َ (يَكْتُبُ → أن يَكْتُبَ), and the 'five verbs' — plural/dual and 2nd-fem-sing — drop their final ن (يَكْتُبونَ → أن يَكْتُبوا). لَنْ is the standard future negation.

drills.translation: I want to learn Arabic well.
I studied so that I would pass the exam.
We traveled in order to see the world.

points.title: Jussive & past negation (لم)
points.function: Negate the past and give commands with 'let'
points.explanation: لَمْ + the jussive negates the past: لَمْ يَذْهَبْ 'he did not go' (present form, past meaning). In the jussive the final ُ becomes sukūn ْ, and the five verbs drop their ن (لم يَذهَبوا). لِـ + jussive gives a third-person command ('let…'): لِيَذْهَبْ 'let him go'.

drills.translation: I did not understand the lesson yesterday.
They did not go to the party.
Why didn't you read my message?
Let each one speak in turn.
We haven't heard the news yet.

points.title: inna and her sisters (إنّ وأخواتها)
points.function: Emphasize, report, and give reasons
points.explanation:
إِنَّ (indeed) and her 'sisters' — أَنَّ (that), لَكِنَّ (but), لِأَنَّ (because), كَأَنَّ (as if), لَعَلَّ (perhaps), لَيْتَ (if only) — begin a nominal sentence and put its subject in the accusative: البيتُ كبيرٌ → إنَّ البيتَ كبيرٌ.

The predicate stays nominative.

drills.translation: Indeed the weather is nice today.
I think that Arabic is important.
He is smart but a little lazy.
I was late because the car broke down.
He looks at me as if he knows me.
Perhaps the result will be good.

points.title: The imperative (الأمر)
points.function: Give direct commands
points.explanation:
The command is built from the jussive by dropping the present prefix and, if that leaves a cluster, adding a helping أ: يَكْتُبُ → اُكْتُبْ 'write!'.

It agrees with the addressee: اُكْتُبْ (to a man), اُكْتُبي (to a woman), اُكْتُبوا (to a group).

Negative commands use لا + jussive (لا تَكْتُبْ 'don't write').

points.title: The verbal noun (المصدر)
points.function: Turn an action into a noun ('reading', 'to travel')
points.explanation:
The maṣdar names the action itself — English 'reading', 'the study of', 'to travel'.

Each verb has one, often on a fixed pattern: قَرَأ → قِراءة (reading), دَرَسَ → دِراسة (study), سافَرَ → سَفَر / مُسافَرة (travel).

It behaves as an ordinary noun (takes ال, iḍāfa, cases) and often replaces an 'أن + verb' clause.

drills.translation: Reading is useful for the mind.
I love traveling to new countries.
Waking up early is good for health.

points.title: Participles (كاتب، مكتوب)
points.function: Say who does an action and what it's done to
points.explanation:
The active participle (ism al-fāʿil) names the doer — كَتَبَ → كاتِب 'writer/writing', on the pattern فاعِل for form-I verbs.

The passive participle (ism al-mafʿūl) names the thing acted upon — كُتِبَ → مَكْتوب 'written', on the pattern مَفْعول.

Both work as nouns or adjectives and agree in gender and number.

drills.translation: He is a famous writer at the newspaper.
The letter is written in Arabic.
The door has been open since morning.
She is a skilled worker at the hospital.
The food is cooked on the fire.
The child is asleep in his bed.

points.title: Relative clauses (الذي، التي، الذين)
points.function: Point out 'the one who / which'
points.explanation:
A definite noun takes a relative pronoun agreeing in gender and number: الَّذي (m.s.), الَّتي (f.s.), الَّذينَ (m.pl.) — الرجلُ الَّذي جاء 'the man who came'.

The clause usually carries a resumptive pronoun referring back (الكتابُ الَّذي قرأتُهُ 'the book that I read it'). After an indefinite noun, no relative pronoun is used at all.

drills.translation: The man who works here is a doctor.
The city that I visited is beautiful.
The students who passed are happy.
This is the book I told you about.
The girl who is singing is talented.
The people who helped me are my friends.

points.title: The passive voice (المبني للمجهول)
points.function: Say an action was done without naming the doer
points.explanation:
The passive changes the verb's vowels, not its letters: past كَتَبَ → كُتِبَ kutiba 'was written'; present يَكْتُب → يُكْتَب yuktab 'is written'.

The doer is dropped and the object becomes the subject (nominative).

In hollow verbs the change is visible: قال qāla 'said' → قيل qīla 'was said', باع → بيع bīʿa 'was sold'.

drills.translation: It was said that the party was postponed.
The old house was sold yesterday.
The child was born in winter.
The winner was announced at the end of the ceremony.
This book was printed in Egypt.
Arabic is used in many countries.

points.title: Derived verb forms II–IV (فعّل، فاعَل، أفعَل)
points.function: Shift a root's meaning: make, do together, cause
points.explanation:
Beyond the base (form I), roots take augmented patterns with new meaning.

Form II فَعَّل (doubled middle) intensifies or makes causative: دَرَسَ 'study' → دَرَّسَ 'teach'.

Form III فاعَل (added alif) is done to/with someone: كَتَبَ → كاتَبَ 'correspond with'.

Form IV أَفْعَل (prefix أ) is causative: خَرَجَ 'go out' → أَخْرَجَ 'take out, produce'.

drills.translation: The professor teaches Arabic to foreigners.
The mother dresses the child every morning.
I correspond with my friend every week by letters.
The audience watches the match with enthusiasm.
The director produced a new film this year.
The company sent the goods abroad.

points.title: Derived verb forms V–X (تفعّل، انفعل، افتعل، استفعل)
points.function: Reflexives, results, and 'seeking' meanings
points.explanation:
Form V تَفَعَّل is the reflexive of II: عَلَّم 'teach' → تَعَلَّم 'learn (for oneself)'.

Form VII اِنْفَعَل is passive/resultative: كَسَر 'break' → اِنْكَسَر 'break (intr.)'.

Form VIII اِفْتَعَل (infixed ت) often reflexive/mutual: جَمَع → اِجْتَمَع 'gather, meet'.

Form X اِسْتَفْعَل means seek or consider: خَدَم → اِسْتَخْدَم 'use', غَفَر → اِسْتَغْفَر 'ask forgiveness'.

drills.translation: The glass broke when it fell.
The manager met with the staff in the morning.

drills.sentence: نحن {{answer}} الحاسوب كل يوم في العمل.
drills.translation: We use the computer every day at work.
drills.transliteration: naḥnu ___ al-ḥāsūb kull yawm fī l-ʿamal

drills.translation: She listens to music before sleeping.
The weather became moderate after the rain.

points.title: The conditional (الشرط: إذا، إن، لو)
points.function: Say 'if… then…' — real and unreal
points.explanation: إذا + past states a likely future condition: إذا دَرَسْتَ نَجَحْتَ 'if you study, you'll pass'. إِنْ + jussive is the formal equivalent: إن تَدْرُسْ تَنْجَحْ. لَوْ + past is counterfactual (it didn't happen), and its answer clause takes لـ: لَوْ دَرَسْتَ لَنَجَحْتَ 'had you studied, you'd have passed'.

drills.translation: If you study hard, you'll pass the exam.
If you travel to Egypt, you'll see the pyramids.
If I were in your place, I would have accepted the offer.
If it rains, we'll stay home.
Had I known the truth, I would have told you.
If you work hard, you'll achieve your dream.

points.title: The circumstantial ḥāl (الحال)
points.function: Describe the state something is in during an action
points.explanation: The ḥāl is an accusative word describing the subject's or object's state while the main action happens — English '…ing' or 'while…': جاء الولدُ راكِضًا 'the boy came running', شَرِبْتُ القهوةَ ساخِنةً 'I drank the coffee hot'. It's indefinite, accusative (ـًا), and agrees with its owner in gender and number.

drills.translation: The teacher entered the class smiling.
The children left the school hurrying.
The soldier stood before the door silent.

points.title: The specification tamyīz (التمييز)
points.function: Clarify 'in what respect' after amounts and comparatives
points.explanation:
The tamyīz is an accusative noun that specifies what a vague amount or comparison refers to — English 'in…', 'of…', '…-wise': اِمْتَلأَ الكوبُ ماءً 'the cup filled with water', اِزْدادَ الطالبُ عِلمًا 'the student grew in knowledge', أنتَ أكثرُ مِنّي مالًا 'you are richer than me in money'.

It's singular, indefinite, accusative.

drills.translation: The man grew in wisdom over the years.
He is greater than me in knowledge and experience.
The student became content (lit. good in soul).
I bought a pound of grapes.
The city grew in population this decade.

points.title: Weak verbs (الأفعال المعتلّة)
points.function: Conjugate verbs whose root has و or ي
points.explanation:
Roots containing و or ي behave irregularly. Assimilated (مثال, initial و): وَصَلَ 'arrive' drops the و in the present — يَصِل.

Hollow (أجوف, middle و/ي): قالَ → يَقول → the vowel shortens in the jussive/command قُلْ.

Defective (ناقص, final و/ي): دَعا → يَدْعو, مَشى → يَمْشي, and the weak letter drops in the jussive: لم يَدْعُ.

drills.translation: The train arrives on time always.
He prays to God every night.
The child didn't walk to school today.
When did you return home yesterday?

points.title: The exception (الاستثناء: إلّا، غير، سوى)
points.function: Say 'all except…' and 'none but…'
points.explanation: إلّا excludes one item from a whole: جاء الطلابُ إلّا واحِدًا 'the students came except one' — the excepted noun is accusative after a full affirmative. In a negative sentence it takes the case its role demands: ما نَجَحَ إلّا طالِبٌ 'only one student passed'. غَيْر and سِوى do the same job as nouns, putting what follows in the genitive: غَيْرُ طالِبٍ.

drills.translation: Everyone attended the meeting except their manager.
I read the whole book except the last chapter.
Only two students remained in the class.
The guests came except my sick friend.

points.title: Vocative & emphasis (النداء والتوكيد بالنون)
points.function: Address people formally and swear emphatic promises
points.explanation:
Address someone with يا: يا طالِبُ (single word, nominative-like), or the formal يا أيُّها + noun for 'O you…': يا أيُّها الناسُ 'O people', يا أيَّتُها السيّدةُ.

For strong emphasis, the energetic نون attaches to the verb after a swearing لـ: لأَكْتُبَنَّ 'I shall certainly write', واللهِ لأَنجَحَنَّ 'by God, I will surely succeed'.

drills.translation: By God, I shall certainly work to achieve my goal.
I will certainly tell the truth whatever the cost.
O madam, please have a seat.
I shall certainly finish the homework before sleeping.

points.title: Fronting & restriction (التقديم، إنّما)
points.function: Move a word forward for emphasis; say 'only'
points.explanation: Arabic's default verb-first order can be reordered for emphasis by fronting (taqdīm): putting the object or a prepositional phrase first highlights it — إيّاكَ نَعْبُدُ 'You alone we worship', في البيتِ رجلٌ 'in the house is a man'. Restriction ('only') is expressed with إِنَّما or with ما … إلّا: إِنَّما المؤمنونَ إخوةٌ 'the believers are but brothers'.

drills.translation: You alone we worship, and You we ask for help.
None succeeded but the diligent one.
On the table is an old book. (fronted phrase)
Truth alone prevails and is not overcome.
To You alone I complain of my sorrow. (fronted)

points.title: Literary discourse (أمّا…فـ، قد، لقد)
points.function: Structure formal prose: 'as for…', emphasis, and completion
points.explanation: Elevated Arabic organizes discourse with fixed devices. أمّا … فـ picks out and comments on a topic: أمّا الطُّلابُ فَمُجْتَهِدونَ 'as for the students, they are diligent' — the فـ is obligatory. قَدْ + past asserts an action definitely happened (قد وَصَلَ 'he has indeed arrived'); لَقَدْ intensifies it. قَدْ + present means 'may/sometimes'.

drills.translation: As for the students, they are diligent; as for the teachers, devoted.
As for the work, we finished it on time.
The guests have indeed arrived a little while ago.
I did work hard all year, so I passed.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} تغيرت المدينة كثيرًا منذ ذلك الحين.
drills.translation: The city has indeed changed a lot since then.
